1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi, OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | — (review owns) | — | **write** (reset at review start) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	## Agent Roles
112	
113	### Plan Agent
114	
115	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
116	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
117	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
118	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
119	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
120	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
121	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
122	
123	### Prover Agent
124	
125	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
126	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
127	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
128	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
129	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
130	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
131	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
132	
133	### Subagents
134	
135	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
136	
137	### Review Agent
138	
139	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
140	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
141	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
142	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
143	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
144	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
145	
146	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
147	
148	These run automatically between phases:
149	
150	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
151	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
152	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Protected declarations
18	
19	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
20	
21	## Avoid early termination
22	
23	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
24	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
25	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
26	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
27	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
28	
29	## Task completion criteria
30	
31	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
32	
33	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
34	2. Zero axioms introduced.
35	3. The file compiles cleanly.
36	
37	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
38	
39	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
40	
41	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
42	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
43	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
44	
45	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
46	
47	## When infrastructure is missing
48	
49	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
50	
51	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
52	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
53	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
54	
55	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
56	
57	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
58	
59	## Proof style
60	
61	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
62	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
63	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
64	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
65	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.
66	
67	## Mathlib tags in PROGRESS.md
68	
69	The plan agent tags suggested lemmas:
70	
71	- `[verified]` — confirmed to exist.
72	- `[expected]` — guessed by naming convention. Quick `lean_local_search`; pivot if it doesn't exist.
73	- `[gap]` — verified NOT in Mathlib. Don't waste search time; formalize a workaround.
74	
75	## LSP MCP tools
76	
77	The `archon-lean-lsp` server exposes Lean LSP operations as **MCP tool calls** (full names like `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_goal` / `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages`). The lean4 skill reference uses short names (`lean_goal`, `lean_local_search`, …) — same tools, NOT shell binaries.
78	
79	- Always invoke through your tool-call interface, the same way as `Read` or `Grep`.
80	- **Never** call `Bash` with `lean_goal …` etc. — there is no such shell command.
81	- First LSP action: `mcp__archon-lean-lsp__lean_diagnostic_messages` on your file. If it returns `success: false` the server is cold; retry once or run `lake build` once via Bash, then retry.
82	
83	## Search protocol
84	
85	Follow `references/lean-lsp-tools-api.md`. Key priority:
86	
87	1. `lean_local_search` first.
88	2. `lean_leansearch` for semantic search — describe the mathematical content, not just the name.
89	3. `lean_loogle` for simple type patterns only.
90	4. Never use shell `find` / `grep` to locate Mathlib theorems.
91	
92	## Tooling traps
93	
94	- **Trust `goals_after`, not just empty diagnostics**: in `lean_multi_attempt`, `diagnostics: []` can be misleading (line-scoping quirks attach errors to the enclosing `by` block). Check that `goals_after` is empty or has advanced.
95	- **Beware `lean_run_code` with imports**: standalone snippets can silently swallow elaboration errors when they contain imports. Rely on the actual file's `lean_diagnostic_messages` for authoritative verification.
96	
97	## Logging
98	
99	Write to `task_results/<your_file>.md` (mirror your `.lean` path: `Algebra/WLocal.lean` → `task_results/Algebra_WLocal.lean.md`):
100	
101	```markdown
102	# Algebra/WLocal.lean
103	
104	## wLocal_iff (line 45)
105	### Attempt 1
106	- **Approach:** Direct case split on maximal ideals
107	- **Result:** FAILED — needed IsLocalRing instance, not available
108	- **Dead end:** direct case split without IsLocalRing
109	
110	### Attempt 2
111	- **Approach:** Stacks 0A31, characterize via bijection on spectra
112	- **Result:** RESOLVED
113	- **Key insight:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_injective` bridges the gap
114	
115	## helper_bijective (line 78)
116	### Attempt 1
117	- **Approach:** Split into injectivity + surjectivity; prove injectivity
118	- **Result:** PARTIAL (branch closed) — injectivity closed; surjectivity remains
119	- **Next step:** `PrimeSpectrum.range_comap_of_surjective` for the right branch
120	- **Lemmas found:** `PrimeSpectrum.comap_surjective`
121	```
122	
123	**Rules**: one section per theorem/lemma in your file. Each attempt records approach, result (RESOLVED / FAILED / PARTIAL / IN PROGRESS), dead-end warnings or next steps. 11	# The identity component of the Picard scheme (A.3)
12	
13	This file is the **A.3** sub-build chapter for the project's positive-genus arm
14	of `nonempty_jacobianWitness`. It scaffolds the abstract identity-component
15	substrate for a `k`-group scheme locally of finite type and specialises it to
16	`G = Pic_{C/k}`, packaging:
17	
18	1. the open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure of the identity component,
19	2. the degree map `Pic_{C/k}(k) → ℤ`,
20	3. the abelian-variety identification of `Pic⁰_{C/k}` (the Jacobian variety
21	   of `C` when `C/k` is a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of
22	   positive genus).
23	
24	## Status (iter-185 NEW file-skeleton)
25	
26	This file is the **iter-185 IdentityComponent** file-skeleton (NEW lane,
27	blueprint-reviewer HARD GATE cleared for the iter-184 plan-phase chapter
28	`Picard_IdentityComponent.tex`). Each of the five blueprint-pinned
29	declarations carries the *intended* substantive type signature (matching the
30	`\lean{...}` pin in `Picard_IdentityComponent.tex`) with a `sorry` body. The
31	bodies are iter-186+ work; iter-185's mandate is the mechanical scaffold only.
32	
33	The 5 pinned declarations are:
34	
35	1. `AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent` (def, ~5 LOC) — the
36	   **identity component** `G^0` of a `k`-group scheme `G` locally of finite
37	   type, as a `k`-scheme (an `Over (Spec k)`-object). Abstract substrate
38	   reusable outside the Picard context; not yet in Mathlib.
39	2. `AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme`
40	   (theorem, ~10 LOC) — the bundled statement that `G^0` is an open and
41	   closed subscheme of `G` via an open immersion `G^0 ↪ G`.
42	3. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme` (def, ~5 LOC) — the **identity
43	   component of the Picard scheme** `Pic⁰_{C/k}`, obtained by applying
44	   `IdentityComponent` to `G = PicScheme C`.
45	4. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.PicScheme.degree` (def, ~5 LOC) — the **degree
46	   map** `Pic_{C/k}(k) → ℤ`, extracting the leading coefficient of the
47	   Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed
48	   degree-one polarisation `O_C(1)`.
49	5. `AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety` (theorem, ~10 LOC)
50	   — the **abelian-variety identification** of `Pic⁰_{C/k}`: smooth, proper,
51	   geometrically irreducible `k`-group scheme of dimension `g(C)` --- the
52	   Jacobian variety of `C`.
53	
54	## Note on type expressivity
55	
56	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof", each
57	pinned declaration carries a substantive, non-tautological type:
58	
59	- `IdentityComponent G : Over (Spec (.of k))` — a `k`-scheme, not the
60	  tautological `G` itself.
61	- `IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme G` — asserts the *existence* of an
62	  open immersion morphism `IdentityComponent G ⟶ G` whose underlying map of
63	  schemes is both an open and a closed immersion (clopen subscheme).
64	- `Pic0Scheme C : Over (Spec (.of k))` — a `k`-scheme.
65	- `PicScheme.degree C : (Spec (.of k) ⟶ (PicScheme C).left) → ℤ` —
66	  a genuine function from `k`-points to `ℤ`, not a constant.
67	- `Pic0Scheme.isAbelianVariety C` — asserts the conjunction of the four
68	  abelian-variety properties (proper, smooth, geometrically irreducible,
69	  group-object structure); not vacuous because each conjunct is a genuine
70	  property/structure on the (typed-sorry) `Pic0Scheme C`.

94	/-! ## §1. The identity component of a group scheme — abstract substrate
95	
96	The identity component is an abstract feature of `k`-group schemes locally of
97	finite type. We package the definition + open-and-closed-subgroup-scheme
98	structure in the `GroupScheme` namespace so they are reusable outside the
99	Picard context.
100	
101	In this project, a "`k`-group scheme" is encoded as an
102	`Over (Spec (.of k))`-object `G` carrying a `[GrpObj G]` instance (the
103	group-object structure in the over-category). The locally-of-finite-type
104	hypothesis is the `[LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom]` instance on the structural
105	morphism to the base.
106	
107	Blueprint references: `def:identity_component_group_scheme` +
108	`thm:identity_component_open_subgroup` (Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`). -/

110	namespace GroupScheme

112	/-- Helper (iter-188): in a **Noetherian** topological space `α`, the set of
113	connected components is finite.
114	
115	Proof: the map `irreducibleComponents α → ConnectedComponents α` sending each
116	irreducible component `C` to the connected component of an arbitrary chosen
117	point of `C` is well-defined (because `C` is preconnected, hence contained in
118	a single connected component) and surjective (because every point lies in
119	some irreducible component, namely `irreducibleComponent x`, which is a
120	subset of `connectedComponent x`). The source is finite by
121	`TopologicalSpace.NoetherianSpace.finite_irreducibleComponents`, so the
122	target is finite by `Finite.of_surjective`. -/
123	private lemma noetherianSpace_finite_connectedComponents
124	    {α : Type*} [TopologicalSpace α] [TopologicalSpace.NoetherianSpace α] :
125	    Finite (ConnectedComponents α) := by
126	  classical
127	  have hfin : (irreducibleComponents α).Finite :=
128	    TopologicalSpace.NoetherianSpace.finite_irreducibleComponents
129	  haveI : Finite ↥(irreducibleComponents α) := hfin.to_subtype
130	  refine Finite.of_surjective
131	    (fun C : ↥(irreducibleComponents α) =>
132	      (ConnectedComponents.mk : α → ConnectedComponents α)
133	        ((C.property : Maximal IsIrreducible C.val).prop.nonempty.some)) ?_
134	  intro c
135	  obtain ⟨x, rfl⟩ := ConnectedComponents.surjective_coe c
136	  refine ⟨⟨irreducibleComponent x, irreducibleComponent_mem_irreducibleComponents x⟩, ?_⟩
137	  rw [ConnectedComponents.coe_eq_coe]
138	  refine (connectedComponent_eq ?_).symm
139	  exact irreducibleComponent_subset_connectedComponent
140	    (isIrreducible_irreducibleComponent (x := x)).nonempty.some_mem
141	
142	/-- Helper (iter-188): in a **Noetherian** topological space `α`, each
143	connected component is open.
144	
145	Proof: `ConnectedComponents α` is totally disconnected
146	(`ConnectedComponents.totallyDisconnectedSpace`), hence T1
147	(`TotallyDisconnectedSpace.t1Space`), and finite by
148	`noetherianSpace_finite_connectedComponents`. A finite T1 space is discrete
149	(`Finite.instDiscreteTopology`), so the singleton
150	`{ConnectedComponents.mk x}` is open in the quotient. The preimage of this
151	singleton under the continuous quotient map `ConnectedComponents.mk` is
152	`connectedComponent x` (`connectedComponents_preimage_singleton`), which is
153	therefore open. -/
154	private lemma noetherianSpace_isOpen_connectedComponent
155	    {α : Type*} [TopologicalSpace α] [TopologicalSpace.NoetherianSpace α]
156	    (x : α) :
157	    IsOpen (connectedComponent x) := by
158	  haveI : Finite (ConnectedComponents α) := noetherianSpace_finite_connectedComponents
159	  haveI : DiscreteTopology (ConnectedComponents α) := inferInstance
160	  have h := (isOpen_discrete
161	    ({(ConnectedComponents.mk x : ConnectedComponents α)} : Set _)).preimage
162	    ConnectedComponents.continuous_coe
163	  rwa [connectedComponents_preimage_singleton] at h
164	
165	/-- Helper (iter-188): the **`LocallyConnectedSpace` instance** for the
166	underlying topological space of a `k`-scheme `G.left` whose structural
167	morphism `G.hom : G.left ⟶ Spec k` is locally of finite type.
168	
169	The substantive content is EGA I 6.1.9: a locally Noetherian topological
170	space has open connected components — equivalently, is locally connected.
171	Pushed through the chain: `Spec k` is Noetherian (a field has Noetherian
172	spectrum); `LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom + IsLocallyNoetherian (Spec k) ⟹
173	IsLocallyNoetherian G.left` (Mathlib's
174	`AlgebraicGeometry.LocallyOfFiniteType.isLocallyNoetherian`); and the
175	implication `IsLocallyNoetherian X ⟹ LocallyConnectedSpace X.toTopCat`
176	is the iter-188 project-side helper (`noetherianSpace_finite_connectedComponents`
177	+ `noetherianSpace_isOpen_connectedComponent`).
178	
179	The classical proof: each point `y ∈ G.left` has an open affine
180	neighbourhood `W = Spec R` with `R` Noetherian, hence `|W|` is a Noetherian
181	topological space; in a Noetherian space, each `connectedComponent y` is
182	clopen (finite irreducible components ⟹ finite connected components ⟹
183	finite T1 quotient ⟹ discrete quotient ⟹ singletons clopen in quotient ⟹
184	preimages clopen). Pulled back along the open inclusion `W ↪ G.left`,
185	this gives an open connected neighbourhood of `y` inside any open `F`
186	containing `y`. -/
187	private instance identityComponent_locallyConnectedSpace
188	    {k : Type u} [Field k]
189	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
190	    [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
191	    LocallyConnectedSpace G.left := by
192	  haveI : IsLocallyNoetherian G.left := LocallyOfFiniteType.isLocallyNoetherian G.hom
193	  rw [locallyConnectedSpace_iff_subsets_isOpen_isConnected]
194	  intro x F hF
195	  -- Extract an open neighbourhood `T ⊆ F` of `x`.
196	  obtain ⟨T, hTF, hT, hxT⟩ := mem_nhds_iff.mp hF
197	  -- Find an affine open `W ⊆ T` with `x ∈ W`.
198	  obtain ⟨W, hW, hxW, hWT⟩ :=
199	    exists_isAffineOpen_mem_and_subset (X := G.left) (U := ⟨T, hT⟩) hxT
200	  haveI : IsNoetherianRing Γ(G.left, W) :=
201	    IsLocallyNoetherian.component_noetherian ⟨W, hW⟩
202	  haveI hNoethW : TopologicalSpace.NoetherianSpace ↥W :=
203	    noetherianSpace_of_isAffineOpen W hW
204	  -- Inside `W` (Noetherian) the connected component of `x` is open; its image
205	  -- under the open inclusion `W ↪ G.left` is the connected open neighbourhood
206	  -- we want.
207	  let xW : ↥W := ⟨x, hxW⟩
208	  refine ⟨(Subtype.val : ↥W → G.left) '' connectedComponent xW, ?_, ?_, ?_, ?_⟩
209	  · rintro _ ⟨z, _, rfl⟩
210	    exact hTF (hWT z.2)
211	  · exact W.isOpen.isOpenMap_subtype_val _
212	      (noetherianSpace_isOpen_connectedComponent xW)
213	  · exact ⟨xW, mem_connectedComponent, rfl⟩
214	  · exact isConnected_connectedComponent.image _ continuous_subtype_val.continuousOn
215	
216	/-- The image of the identity section `e : Spec k → G` (well-defined as a
217	single point of `|G|` because `Spec k` is a topological singleton). -/
218	private noncomputable def identitySectionPoint
219	    {k : Type u} [Field k] (G : Over (Spec (.of k))) [GrpObj G] : G.left :=
220	  ((MonObj.one (X := G)).left.base :
221	      ↥(Spec (.of k)) → G.left) (default : Spec (.of k))
222	
223	/-- Helper (iter-186; iter-187 closed): the **clopen carrier `Set`** of
224	the identity component of a `k`-group scheme `G` locally of finite type,

228	needs the `identityComponent_locallyConnectedSpace` instance above
229	(EGA I 6.1.9). -/
230	private noncomputable def identityComponentCarrier {k : Type u} [Field k]
231	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
232	    [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
233	    G.left.Opens :=
234	  ⟨connectedComponent (identitySectionPoint G), isOpen_connectedComponent⟩

240	intended substantive identity "this is the connected component of `|G|`

246	iter-186 body: built from the `identityComponentCarrier G` helper — the

249	structure morphism is the inherited `(identityComponentCarrier G).ι ≫ G.hom`.
250	The substantive content (the actual carrier `Set`, plus its openness from

253	noncomputable def IdentityComponent {k : Type u} [Field k]
254	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
255	    [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
256	    Over (Spec (.of k)) :=
257	  Over.mk ((identityComponentCarrier G).ι ≫ G.hom)

259	/-- **The identity component is an open and closed subgroup scheme.**
260	
261	The bundled statement of Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`~(3): the identity
262	component `G^0 = IdentityComponent G` of a `k`-group scheme `G` locally of
263	finite type comes with a morphism `IdentityComponent G ⟶ G` (in
264	`Over (Spec (.of k))`) whose underlying scheme morphism is both an open
265	immersion and a closed immersion (i.e. the inclusion of a clopen
266	subscheme).
267	
268	The full Kleiman conclusion also packages the group-subscheme property (the
269	inclusion is a homomorphism of `k`-group schemes), finite-type-ness over `k`
270	(`LocallyOfFiniteType` + quasi-compactness), geometric irreducibility, and
271	base-change-commutation. Those refinements live as separate instances /
272	follow-up lemmas in iter-186+; the file-skeleton pins only the clopen
273	open-immersion conclusion as a Nonempty-witness.
274	
275	iter-186 body: the inclusion morphism is `Over.homMk (identityComponentCarrier G).ι`,
276	with the over-category compatibility holding by definition of
277	`IdentityComponent G`. The `.left` of this morphism is
278	`(identityComponentCarrier G).ι`, an open immersion by the global
279	`Scheme.Opens.instIsOpenImmersionι` instance. For the closed-immersion
280	half we apply `IsClosedImmersion.of_isPreimmersion` to the open immersion
281	and reduce to `IsClosed (↑(identityComponentCarrier G) : Set _)`. The
282	remaining inline `sorry` is the substantive content: the carrier of
283	`identityComponentCarrier G` is closed, by "closure of a connected subspace
284	is connected" applied to the connected component through the image of the
285	identity section. -/
286	theorem IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme {k : Type u} [Field k]
287	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
288	    [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
289	    Nonempty {f : IdentityComponent G ⟶ G //
290	        IsOpenImmersion f.left ∧ IsClosedImmersion f.left} := by
291	  -- Over-category compatibility for the underlying open immersion.
292	  have hcomp : (identityComponentCarrier G).ι ≫ G.hom = (IdentityComponent G).hom := by
293	    simp [IdentityComponent]
294	  -- The over-morphism witness.
295	  let f : IdentityComponent G ⟶ G :=
296	    Over.homMk (U := IdentityComponent G) (V := G) (identityComponentCarrier G).ι hcomp
297	  refine ⟨⟨f, ?_, ?_⟩⟩
298	  · -- `f.left = (identityComponentCarrier G).ι` (definitionally; `Over.homMk_left` simp).
299	    -- An open immersion by `Scheme.Opens.instIsOpenImmersionι`.
300	    change IsOpenImmersion (identityComponentCarrier G).ι
301	    infer_instance
302	  · -- `f.left = (identityComponentCarrier G).ι` is a preimmersion (open immersion ⟹
303	    -- immersion ⟹ preimmersion). Its range is the carrier set (`Scheme.Opens.range_ι`),
304	    -- and closure of that connected subset is itself (Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`~(3):
305	    -- the connected component through the identity is closed in `|G|`).
306	    change IsClosedImmersion (identityComponentCarrier G).ι
307	    apply IsClosedImmersion.of_isPreimmersion
308	    rw [Scheme.Opens.range_ι]
309	    -- The carrier set is `connectedComponent (identitySectionPoint G)`, which is
310	    -- closed by `isClopen_connectedComponent.1` (with the
311	    -- `identityComponent_locallyConnectedSpace` instance providing the closedness
312	    -- half of EGA I 6.1.9: connected components of a locally Noetherian topological
313	    -- space are clopen).
314	    change IsClosed (connectedComponent (identitySectionPoint G))
315	    exact isClopen_connectedComponent.1
316	
317	/-- **The identity component inclusion is a group-scheme homomorphism.**
318	
319	Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`~(3) conclusion (b): the clopen subscheme `G^0`
320	inherits a `k`-group-scheme structure from `G`, and the inclusion morphism
321	`G^0 ↪ G` (from `IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme`) is compatible
322	with the group laws on source and target. The statement-level pin asserts
323	the existence of the inherited `GrpObj` structure; the compatibility of
324	the inclusion with the group operations is the substantive content of
325	Kleiman's argument (the product `G^0 ×_k G^0` is connected by
326	EGA IV₂ 4.5.8; the group-multiplication map sends this connected subset
327	containing the identity into the connected component `G^0`).
328	
329	The full statement (existence of `GrpObj (IdentityComponent G)` *together*
330	with the homomorphism-compatibility of the inclusion in the relevant
331	`Hom`-form) lives in iter-188+. -/
332	theorem IdentityComponent.isSubgroupHomomorphism {k : Type u} [Field k]
333	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
334	    [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
335	    Nonempty (GrpObj (IdentityComponent G)) :=
336	  sorry
337	
338	/-- **The identity component is of finite type and geometrically irreducible.**
339	
340	Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`~(3) conclusion (c): the open subgroup `G^0` of
341	a `k`-group scheme `G` locally of finite type is itself
342	locally-of-finite-type-plus-quasi-compact (i.e., of finite type) over `k`,
343	and is geometrically irreducible. The proof reduces (after base change to
344	`\bar k`) to picking a nonempty smooth affine open subset `U ⊆ G^0`; the
345	translates `hg⁻¹U` give an open cover of `G^0` by smooth, hence irreducible,
346	neighbourhoods, so `G^0` is locally irreducible at every closed point; with
347	connectedness this gives irreducibility globally (EGA I 6.1.10). The image
348	`α(U × U) = G^0` is the image of the affine, hence quasi-compact, scheme
349	`U × U`, so `G^0` is quasi-compact.
350	
351	iter-189 partial progress: the `LocallyOfFiniteType` conjunct closes
352	axiom-clean via composition of an open immersion with `G.hom`. The
353	remaining two conjuncts (`QuasiCompact`, `GeometricallyIrreducible`)
354	require the group-structure argument and are bundled into the residual
355	sorry. -/
356	theorem IdentityComponent.isFiniteTypeGeometricallyIrreducible
357	    {k : Type u} [Field k]
358	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k)))
359	    [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom] :
360	    LocallyOfFiniteType (IdentityComponent G).hom ∧
361	      QuasiCompact (IdentityComponent G).hom ∧
362	      GeometricallyIrreducible (IdentityComponent G).hom := by
363	  refine ⟨?_, ?_⟩
364	  · -- `LocallyOfFiniteType`: `(IdentityComponent G).hom` unfolds to
365	    -- `(identityComponentCarrier G).ι ≫ G.hom`. Open immersion ∘ LFT
366	    -- is LFT (`locallyOfFiniteType_of_isOpenImmersion` +
367	    -- `locallyOfFiniteType_comp`).
368	    change LocallyOfFiniteType ((identityComponentCarrier G).ι ≫ G.hom)
369	    infer_instance
370	  · -- `QuasiCompact ∧ GeometricallyIrreducible`: Kleiman's group-theoretic
371	    -- argument. `α(U × U) = G^0` quasi-compact via the affine `U × U`;
372	    -- geometric irreducibility via base change to `\bar k`, picking a
373	    -- smooth affine `U ⊆ G^0`, and translating `hg⁻¹U` to cover. Cannot
374	    -- close axiom-clean without the substrate `isSubgroupHomomorphism`
375	    -- + EGA IV₂ 4.5.8 / 4.6.1 / EGA I 6.1.10 (not yet in Mathlib).
376	    sorry

379	
380	Kleiman §5 Lem.~`lem:agps`~(3) conclusion (d): for any field extension
381	`K/k`, the natural comparison map `(G^0)_K → (G_K)^0` is an isomorphism of
382	`K`-schemes. The proof: `G^0` is geometrically connected as a connected
383	scheme with a `k`-rational point (EGA IV₂ 4.5.14), so its base change
384	`(G^0)_K` remains connected; it is also open and closed in `G_K`, so
385	coincides with the identity component `(G_K)^0`.
386	
387	The statement-level pin asserts existence of: a `K`-group-scheme structure
388	on the base change `G ×_{Spec k} Spec K` (with appropriate
389	locally-of-finite-type instance), and an isomorphism on underlying schemes
390	identifying the two iterated constructions. The iso witness is a typed

392	provided by `Scheme.GrpObjAsOverPullback` and is propagated through. -/
393	theorem IdentityComponent.baseChangeIso {k : Type u} [Field k]
394	    (G : Over (Spec (.of k))) [GrpObj G] [LocallyOfFiniteType G.hom]
395	    (K : Type u) [Field K] [Algebra k K] :
396	    let φ : Spec (CommRingCat.of K) ⟶ Spec (CommRingCat.of k) :=
397	      Spec.map (CommRingCat.ofHom (algebraMap k K))

399	      Over.mk (CategoryTheory.Limits.pullback.snd G.hom φ)

401	                 (_locFTInst : LocallyOfFiniteType G_K.hom),

403	        CategoryTheory.Limits.pullback (IdentityComponent G).hom φ) :=

442	/-! ## §3. The degree map
443	
444	The disjoint-union structure of `Pic_{C/k}` (a disjoint union of open
445	quasi-projective `k`-subschemes, indexed by Hilbert polynomial via
446	`PicScheme.smoothProperQuotient`) stratifies its `T`-points by the leading
447	coefficient of the Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf
448	relative to a fixed degree-one polarisation. On `k`-points this gives the
449	**degree map** `Pic_{C/k}(k) → ℤ`.
450	
451	Blueprint reference: `def:divisor_degree_pic` (Milne III.1, p.~88). -/
452	
453	namespace PicScheme
454	
455	/-- The **degree map** `Pic_{C/k}(k) → ℤ`.
456	
457	Sends a `k`-point `λ ∈ Pic_{C/k}(k)` --- a morphism
458	`Spec k ⟶ (PicScheme C).left` --- to the leading coefficient of the
459	Hilbert polynomial of a representing invertible sheaf `L` on `C` (relative
460	to a fixed degree-one polarisation `O_C(1)`). By Riemann--Roch,
461	`χ(C, L ⊗ O_C(n)) = n · deg L + 1 - g`, so the degree is the leading
462	coefficient of `Φ_L(n)`, well-defined on the isomorphism class `[L]` and on
463	the `k`-point `λ` (because `PicScheme C` represents the étale-sheafified
464	relative Picard functor).
465	
466	The degree map is a group homomorphism for the additive structure on
467	`Pic_{C/k}(k)` (tensor product on `L`) and the standard `(ℤ, +)`. The full
468	group-homomorphism refinement / functoriality in `k` lives as a follow-up
469	lemma in iter-186+; the file-skeleton pins only the underlying function.
470	
471	iter-186+: the body extracts the representing invertible sheaf from
472	`(PicScheme.representable C)`, forms its Hilbert polynomial via the project's
473	Hilbert-polynomial machinery (sibling `Picard/QuotScheme.lean`), and returns
474	the leading coefficient. For the iter-185 file-skeleton the body is a typed
475	`sorry`. -/
476	noncomputable def degree {k : Type u} [Field k]
477	    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
478	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
479	    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom] :
480	    (Spec (.of k) ⟶ (PicScheme C).left) → ℤ :=
481	  sorry
482	
483	end PicScheme

485	/-! ## §4. `Pic⁰_{C/k}` is an abelian variety
486	
487	The terminal statement of the chapter identifies `Pic⁰_{C/k}` with an
488	abelian variety of dimension `g(C)` --- the Jacobian variety of `C`. In
489	this project, "abelian variety" is the conjunction of the four properties
490	`[GrpObj A] [IsProper A.hom] [Smooth A.hom] [GeometricallyIrreducible A.hom]`
491	threaded through `AlgebraicJacobian.AbelianVarietyRigidity` and consumed by
492	`AlgebraicJacobian.Albanese.AlbaneseUP`.
493	
494	Blueprint reference: `thm:pic_zero_is_abelian_variety` (Kleiman §5
495	Ex.~`ex:jac` + Rmk.~`rmk:Jac`; cf. Milne §I.1, Rmk. III.1.4(e)). -/
496	
497	namespace Pic0Scheme
498	
499	/-- **`Pic⁰_{C/k}` is an abelian variety.**
500	
501	For `C/k` a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus `g = g(C)`
502	over a field `k`, the identity component `Pic⁰_{C/k}` of the Picard scheme
503	is an *abelian variety over* `k`: a smooth, proper, geometrically
504	irreducible `k`-group scheme. (Commutativity follows automatically from
505	Milne §I.1, Cor. 1.4, and is not separately stated here.)
506	
507	The bundled statement: the four pieces `[IsProper] ∧ [Smooth] ∧
508	[GeometricallyIrreducible] ∧ Nonempty (GrpObj _)` hold on
509	`(Pic0Scheme C).hom` and `(Pic0Scheme C)` respectively.
510	
511	Dimension `g = g(C)`: not stated separately at the file-skeleton level
512	(it requires `dim_k (Pic0Scheme C).left = AlgebraicGeometry.genus C`, which
513	involves the Krull-dimension API on the underlying scheme); iter-186+
514	follow-up.
515	
516	iter-186+: the body assembles the four conjuncts from
517	- `GroupScheme.IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme` (clopen subscheme of
518	  `PicScheme C`),
519	- Kleiman §5 Thm.~`th:qpp&p` (`X/k` projective + geom. integral ⟹
520	  `Pic⁰_{X/k}` quasi-projective; upgrade to projective when geom. normal,
521	  which holds for smooth proper curves of positive genus),
522	- Kleiman §5 Cor.~`cor:sm` + Ex.~`ex:jac` (smoothness of `Pic_{X/k}` at the
523	  identity for smooth proper curves; hence smooth of dimension
524	  `dim_k H¹(C, O_C) = g(C)` everywhere by uniform smoothness on the open
525	  identity component).
526	For the iter-185 file-skeleton the body is a typed `sorry`. -/
527	theorem isAbelianVariety {k : Type u} [Field k]
528	    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
529	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
530	    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom] :
531	    IsProper (Pic0Scheme C).hom ∧ Smooth (Pic0Scheme C).hom ∧
532	      GeometricallyIrreducible (Pic0Scheme C).hom ∧
533	      Nonempty (GrpObj (Pic0Scheme C)) :=
534	  sorry
535	
536	/-- **Dimension of `Pic⁰_{C/k}` equals the genus of `C`.**
537	
538	Milne~§III.1, Rmk.~1.4(e): "The dimension of `J` is the genus of `C`".
539	For a smooth proper geometrically integral curve `C/k` of genus
540	`g = g(C)`, the topological Krull dimension of the underlying scheme of
541	`Pic⁰_{C/k}` equals `g(C)`. By Kleiman~§5 Cor.~`cor:sm`, the inequality
542	`dim Pic_{C/k} ≤ dim_k H¹(C, O_C)` is always an equality at points where
543	`Pic_{C/k}` is smooth, and for smooth proper curves
544	(`SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom`) the identity component is smooth
545	(Kleiman~§5 Ex.~`ex:jac`), so the dimension equals `dim_k H¹(C, O_C) = g(C)`
546	by `def:genus`. -/
547	theorem finrank_eq_genus {k : Type u} [Field k]
548	    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
549	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
550	    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom] :
551	    topologicalKrullDim (Pic0Scheme C).left = (AlgebraicGeometry.genus C : WithBot ℕ∞) :=
552	  sorry
553	
554	/-- **`k`-points of `Pic⁰_{C/k}` are the kernel of the degree map.**
555	
556	Milne~§III.1, p.~88: `Pic⁰(C)` is the group of isomorphism classes of
557	invertible sheaves of degree zero on `C`. For a smooth proper geometrically
558	integral curve `C/k`, a `k`-point `λ ∈ Pic_{C/k}(k)` lies in the image of
559	the inclusion `Pic⁰_{C/k} ↪ Pic_{C/k}` (the inclusion of
560	`def:pic_zero_subscheme`, packaged here via the existence of the
561	inclusion morphism) if and only if `degree C λ = 0`.
562	
563	The statement-level pin packages two pieces: existence of the inclusion
564	morphism `Pic⁰_{C/k} ⟶ Pic_{C/k}` (extracted from
565	`IdentityComponent.isOpenSubgroupScheme` once `PicScheme C` has the
566	`GrpObj` + `LocallyOfFiniteType` instances), together with the
567	characterisation of `k`-points factoring through it as those with degree
568	zero. -/
569	theorem kPoints_iff_kerDegree {k : Type u} [Field k]
570	    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
571	    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
572	    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom] :
573	    Nonempty (Σ' (inc : (Pic0Scheme C).left ⟶ (PicScheme C).left),
574	      ∀ (lambda : Spec (.of k) ⟶ (PicScheme C).left),
575	        (∃ mu : Spec (.of k) ⟶ (Pic0Scheme C).left, mu ≫ inc = lambda) ↔
576	          PicScheme.degree C lambda = 0) :=
577	  sorry

1	# Analogy: Identity-component substrate — `G^0 × G^0` connectedness and `GrpObj` inheritance
2	
3	## Mode
4	cross-domain-inspiration
5	
6	## Slug
7	lane-a3i-isconnected-prod
8	
9	## Iteration
10	191
11	
12	## Structural problem (abstracted)
13	
14	For a group object `G` in `Sch/k` with connected identity component `G^0 ⊂ G`
15	carrying a `k`-rational point (the identity section), we need (i) the
16	underlying topological space of the fiber-product `G^0 ×_k G^0` to be
17	connected (substrate for showing the multiplication morphism restricts to
18	`G^0 → G^0 → G^0`), and (ii) the structure of a group object on `G^0`
19	inherited from `G` via an open immersion. Sub-question (i) is the
20	EGA IV₂ 4.5.8 step that has blocked four iters of project work; sub-question
21	(ii) is a bridging issue between the project's `Over.mk (pullback.snd _ _)`
22	shape and Mathlib's `asOver M S` shape via `OverClass`.
23	
24	## Failed approaches (from directive)
25	
26	- iter-186/187: scaffold `isSubgroupHomomorphism` as a sorry, defer to
27	  helpers. Blocked because the helper itself needs the connected-product
28	  substrate, which is the actual gap.
29	- iter-188: full topology-side closure of `identityComponent_locallyConnectedSpace`
30	  via EGA I 6.1.9 (locally Noetherian ⟹ locally connected). Succeeded
31	  because that step is purely topological. The 4.5.8 step is geometric
32	  (needs a hypothesis upgrading "connected" to "geometrically connected"),
33	  so the topology-side recipe doesn't extend.
34	- iter-189: explicit `change` unfold of `(IdentityComponent G).hom` cleared
35	  `LocallyOfFiniteType` inline. The other two conjuncts (`QuasiCompact`,
36	  `GeometricallyIrreducible`) require the group-action argument, which
37	  needs the substrate.
38	
39	## Analogues found
40	
41	Ranked by porting cost (lowest first).
42	
43	### Analogue: `Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Geometrically.Connected` — the `ConnectedSpace (pullback f g)` instance
44	
45	- **Domain**: algebraic geometry / scheme theory (same general area, different
46	  sub-file). Cite: `Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Geometrically/Connected.lean:100-102`
47	  at SHA b80f22719410.
48	
49	- **Same structural problem there**: Given a morphism `f : X ⟶ S` of schemes
50	  that is geometrically connected and universally open, and another morphism
51	  `g : Y ⟶ S` whose source `Y` is a connected topological space, the
52	  scheme-theoretic fiber product `X ×ₛ Y` is again a connected topological
53	  space. This is the direct algebro-geometric realization of EGA IV₂ 4.5.8
54	  ("product of geometrically connected schemes is connected").
55	
56	- **Technique**: The instance is one rewriting step away from the lemma
57	  `GeometricallyConnected.connectedSpace` (same file, line 89), which says
58	  that a geometrically connected morphism with open underlying map and
59	  connected target has a connected source. The pullback case is obtained
60	  from this lemma by base-changing along `g`: the second projection
61	  `pullback.snd : X ×ₛ Y ⟶ Y` inherits `GeometricallyConnected` from `f`
62	  (line 53, stability under base change), inherits `IsOpenMap` because
63	  pullback of universally-open is open (`UniversallyOpen.snd`, line 86 of
64	  `UniversallyOpen.lean`), and `Y` is the connected target. So the instance
65	  is two lines of inferInstance plumbing on top of the
66	  `GeometricallyConnected.connectedSpace` lemma — which itself is a
67	  three-line preimage-of-connected argument.
68	
69	- **Mapping to project**: Take `f = g = (IdentityComponent G).hom : G^0 ⟶ Spec k`.
70	  Specialize to `Y = G^0` (which the project already exhibits as a
71	  `ConnectedSpace` because `(identityComponentCarrier G)` is defined as the
72	  topological connected component of the identity point and the open-subscheme
73	  topology coincides with the subspace topology). The instance then
74	  produces `ConnectedSpace ↥(pullback (IdentityComponent G).hom (IdentityComponent G).hom)`,
75	  which **is** the substrate needed for `isSubgroupHomomorphism`.
76	
77	- **Premises the project still has to discharge**:
78	  1. `UniversallyOpen (IdentityComponent G).hom`. This auto-derives from
79	     `Mathlib/AlgebraicGeometry/Morphisms/UniversallyOpen.lean:149-150`,
80	     which declares `[IsIntegral Y] [Subsingleton Y] → UniversallyOpen f`
81	     as a low-priority instance. Spec of a field is integral and a
82	     subsingleton, so both premises hold for `Y = Spec (.of k)`. **Zero
83	     project-side LOC** — `inferInstance` should fire.
84	  2. `ConnectedSpace ↥(IdentityComponent G).left`. Comes free from the
85	     definition `(identityComponentCarrier G) = connectedComponent ⟨identitySectionPoint G⟩`
86	     plus the standard `Scheme.Opens.instIsOpenImmersionι` topology
87	     coincidence. **~5 project-side LOC** to assemble the instance.
88	  3. **`GeometricallyConnected (IdentityComponent G).hom`** — this is the
89	     genuine substrate gap. Mathlib does NOT have a "connected scheme with
90	     a k-rational section ⟹ geometrically connected" lemma at b80f227
91	     (EGA IV₂ 4.5.14 / Stacks 04KU). See "Porting cost" below.
92	
93	- **Porting cost**: medium. The Mathlib substrate is already in place — the
94	  pullback-connected instance is FREE. The remaining work is **one**
95	  project-side helper, `geometricallyConnected_of_connected_of_section`, of
96	  shape: given `f : X ⟶ Spec k` with `ConnectedSpace X` and a morphism
97	  `s : Spec k ⟶ X` with `s ≫ f = 𝟙 _`, conclude `GeometricallyConnected f`.
98	  Realistic estimate: **80–120 LOC** (matches the iter-189 prover's original
99	  ~80-150 LOC estimate, but here the LOC is concentrated in ONE clean
100	  abstract helper instead of spread across pullback-connectedness machinery
101	  the project would have had to build itself). The classical proof
102	  (Stacks 04KV) descends a hypothetical disconnection of `X_K` to a
103	  disconnection of `X` by exploiting the section: a clopen partition
104	  `X_K = U ⊔ V` would force the K-rational point pulled from the section to
105	  land in exactly one of `U, V`, and the image in `X` would be a clopen
106	  partition whose nonemptiness is witnessed by the section. The proof is
107	  short on paper but needs Mathlib bridging for "base-change clopen
108	  partition descends along surjective faithfully-flat" — that is the bulk
109	  of the LOC.
110	
111	- **Verdict**: ANALOGUE_FOUND.

113	### Analogue: `Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Monoidal.Cartesian.Over.grpObjMkPullbackSnd`
114	
115	- **Domain**: category theory (cartesian monoidal). Cite:
116	  `Mathlib/CategoryTheory/Monoidal/Cartesian/Over.lean:311-313` at SHA
117	  b80f22719410.
118	
119	- **Same structural problem there**: Given a morphism `f : X ⟶ S` such that
120	  `Over.mk f` carries a `GrpObj` instance, the pullback morphism
121	  `pullback.snd f g : pullback f g ⟶ T` (for any `g : T ⟶ S`) carries a
122	  `GrpObj` instance on `Over.mk (pullback.snd f g)`. This is the
123	  base-change-preserves-group-object statement, stated in maximal
124	  generality (any cartesian monoidal category with pullbacks; specializing
125	  to `Sch` is automatic).
126	
127	- **Technique**: The construction goes through the
128	  `(Over.pullback g).mapGrp.obj` functor, which is the categorical
129	  formulation of "base change preserves group objects" via the
130	  functoriality of `mapGrp` (the functor from grouplike objects in one
131	  cartesian category to grouplike objects in another, induced by a
132	  product-preserving functor). The proof is one line of functor-image
133	  unfolding — no scheme-specific content.
134	
135	- **Mapping to project**: The project's `IdentityComponent.baseChangeIso`
136	  needs `GrpObj G_K` for `G_K := Over.mk (pullback.snd G.hom φ)`. The
137	  iter-190 directive worried that Mathlib's
138	  `Scheme.GrpObjAsOverPullback` (`Pullbacks.lean:808`) is stated in terms
139	  of `asOver M S` rather than `Over.mk`, and would require explicit
140	  `OverClass` adapters to consume. **The directive's worry is incorrect** —
141	  Mathlib has the more elementary lemma `grpObjMkPullbackSnd` at the
142	  category-theory level whose conclusion is *literally*
143	  `GrpObj (Over.mk (pullback.snd f g))`, the project's exact shape. The
144	  scheme-side `GrpObjAsOverPullback` is just the `asOver`-wrapped
145	  re-export of this; consuming `grpObjMkPullbackSnd` directly avoids the
146	  OverClass bridging entirely.
147	
148	- **Porting cost**: low. ~10-20 LOC to apply the lemma directly in
149	  `baseChangeIso`: `instance grpObjGK : GrpObj G_K := grpObjMkPullbackSnd`
150	  (with appropriate explicit binding of `f = G.hom`, `g = φ`). The
151	  premise `[GrpObj (Over.mk G.hom)]` is satisfied because `G` itself is
152	  an `Over (Spec k)` and `Over.mk G.hom = G` definitionally for any `G`
153	  constructed via `Over.mk` (which the project does throughout). If
154	  the project's `G` is *not* constructed via `Over.mk` in the relevant
155	  call site, a single `Over.mk G.hom = G` defeq or `Over.isoMk_refl`
156	  rewrite bridges them.
157	
158	- **Verdict**: ANALOGUE_FOUND.

160	### Analogue: `Mathlib.Topology.Algebra.Group.Basic.Subgroup.connectedComponentOfOne`
161	
162	- **Domain**: topology / topological groups (distant from the project's
163	  scheme setting, but EXACT same structural shape: connected component of
164	  identity in a group object yields a sub-group-object).
165	  Cite: `Mathlib/Topology/Algebra/Group/Basic.lean:740-745` (the definition)
166	  and `:718-727` (the multiplication-stability proof
167	  `mul_mem_connectedComponent_one`) and `:729-736` (the inverse-stability
168	  proof `inv_mem_connectedComponent_one`), at SHA b80f22719410.
169	
170	- **Same structural problem there**: For a topological group `G`, the
171	  connected component of the identity is a subgroup. The closure operations
172	  (multiplication, inversion) preserve it.
173	
174	- **Technique** — this is the *interesting* analogue, because Mathlib's
175	  proof technique *sidesteps* the "product of two connected sets is
176	  connected" question entirely. For multiplication, instead of arguing
177	  "(conn cpt of 1) × (conn cpt of 1) is connected, its image under mul
178	  is connected, contains 1, hence ⊆ (conn cpt of 1)", Mathlib uses
179	  ONE-COORDINATE-AT-A-TIME continuity. The proof of
180	  `mul_mem_connectedComponent_one` (line 719) takes `g, h ∈ cc 1` and:
181	  rewrites `cc 1 = cc g` (using `g ∈ cc 1`); shows `g ∈ cc (g*h)` by
182	  applying continuity of `x ↦ g·x` (a single-variable continuous map!) to
183	  the path inside `cc h` from `1` to `h`, producing a path inside `cc (g*h)`
184	  from `g·1 = g` to `g·h`; concludes `cc g = cc (g*h)` and hence
185	  `g*h ∈ cc g = cc 1`. Mul stability without ever forming a product
186	  topology argument.
187	
188	- **Mapping to project**: The technique **does not directly port to the
189	  scheme setting**. Reason: in topology, the connected component of a point
190	  is a subset of the underlying space, so "(g, h) ↦ g·h sends cc(1) × cc(1)
191	  into cc(1)" is a set-theoretic claim about pointwise multiplication on the
192	  underlying space. In the scheme setting, the corresponding set-theoretic
193	  claim would be about `|G^0| × |G^0|` and pointwise multiplication on `|G|`
194	  — but the underlying topological space of the *scheme* `G^0 ×_k G^0` is
195	  strictly larger than `|G^0| × |G^0|` over non-algebraically-closed `k`
196	  (it contains additional "generic" points whose images in `|G|` do not
197	  factor through pointwise mul). So the one-coordinate-continuity trick
198	  cannot replace the substrate. **But the analogue still gives the project
199	  an architectural insight**: define the project's `GrpObj G^0` via
200	  *carrier-level* multiplication-stability and *scheme-level* base change
201	  separately, treating "G^0 is closed under mul as a set in |G|" and
202	  "G^0 ×_k G^0 → G factors through G^0 as a scheme morphism" as separate
203	  obligations. The set-level obligation IS closable by the topological-group
204	  trick (the path on `|G|` from `e·e` to `(e·h)` runs through `e ∈ |G^0|`'s
205	  open neighbourhoods and stays in `|G^0|` because `|G^0|` is the connected
206	  component of `e`). The scheme-level obligation reduces to the
207	  substrate analogue above.
208	
209	- **Porting cost**: high. The technique is structurally beautiful but does
210	  not eliminate the substrate need in the scheme case. **The value is
211	  *architectural***: if the project is willing to refactor
212	  `isSubgroupHomomorphism` to separately establish (a) the set-theoretic
213	  closure of |G^0| under |μ| (which the topological-group trick CAN
214	  provide; this is what `mul_mem_connectedComponent_one` literally proves
215	  about |G| viewed as a topological space carrying a topological-group
216	  structure inherited from the scheme), and (b) the scheme-level factoring
217	  through G^0, the project gets (a) for ~20 LOC by direct port and (b) is
218	  what the substrate analogue above closes. The net cost is the same; the
219	  win is conceptual clarity (the burden is concentrated on the substrate
220	  alone, not entangled with mul-stability).
221	
222	- **Verdict**: PARTIAL_ANALOGUE — technique informs architecture but
223	  cannot replace the substrate.
224	
225	## Top suggestion
226	
227	The planner should prioritize the **first analogue**:

29	\section{Setup and motivation}
30	\label{sec:pic0_setup}
31	
32	Throughout this chapter, fix a base field \(k\) and a smooth proper
33	geometrically integral curve \(C\) over \(k\) of genus \(g = g(C)\)
34	(\cref{def:genus}). By
35	\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability,thm:pic_is_group_scheme}, the
36	\'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor of \(C/k\) is represented by
37	the \(k\)-group scheme \(\Pic_{C/k}\) (\cref{def:pic_scheme}), which is
38	separated, locally of finite type over \(k\), and a disjoint union of
39	open quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes. The disjoint-union structure of
40	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) stratifies its \(T\)-points by the Hilbert polynomial of the
41	representing invertible sheaf relative to a fixed polarisation, and on
42	the geometric points of the curve this stratification reduces to the
43	degree of a line bundle.
44	
45	The identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) singled out by Kleiman is the open
46	and closed subgroup scheme consisting of the connected component of
47	\(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing the trivial line bundle. It is the locus of
48	degree-zero classes, and on a smooth proper curve it is itself an
49	abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\). This
50	chapter packages those four statements as the A.3 specification.
51	
52	\section{The identity component of a group scheme}
53	\label{sec:identity_component_abstract}
54	
55	The identity component is an abstract feature of group schemes locally
56	of finite type over a field. We record the definition and its
57	open-and-closed subgroup-scheme structure separately, so that the
58	Lean substrate
59	\texttt{AlgebraicGeometry.GroupScheme.IdentityComponent} is reusable
60	outside the Picard context.

92	  the open subscheme structure of \cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}.

168	\begin{theorem}

170	\leanok
171	[Formation of the identity component commutes with base change]
172	  \label{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}

174	
175	  % SOURCE: [Kleiman], ``The Picard scheme'', \S 5, Lem.~lem:agps~(3);

190	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: (from references/kleiman-picard-src/kleiman-picard.tex,
191	% L2864-L2911, proof of lem:agps; we reproduce the part dealing with
192	% (3).)
193	% "Consider (3).  By definition, \(G^0\) is the largest connected subspace
194	% containing \(e\).  But the closure of a connected subspace is plainly
195	% connected; so \(G^0\) is closed.  By \cite[6.1.9]{EGAI}, in any locally
196	% Noetherian topological space, the connected components are open.  Thus
197	% \(G^0\) is open too.
198	%
199	% Since \(G^0\) is connected and has a \(k\)-point, \(G^0\) is geometrically
200	% connected by \cite[4.5.14]{EGAIV2}.  Thus forming \(G^0\) commutes with

202	%
203	% Furthermore, \(G^0\x G^0\) is connected by \cite[4.5.8]{EGAIV2}.
204	% So \(\alpha(G^0\x G^0)\subset G\) is connected, and contains \(e\),
205	% so lies in \(G^0\).  Thus \(G^0\) is a subgroup.
206	%
207	% To prove \(G^0\) is geometrically irreducible and quasi-compact, we may
208	% replace \(k\) by its algebraic closure.  Then \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) is
209	% reduced by \cite[4.6.1]{EGAIV2}.  Hence \(\alpha\) induces a map from
210	% \(G^0_{\red}\x G^0_{\red}\) into \(G^0_{\red}\).  So \(G^0_{\red}\) is a
211	% subgroup.  Thus we may replace \(G^0\) by \(G^0_{\red}\).
212	% [\dots]
213	% Hence \(G^0\) is irreducible locally at \(h\).  But \(h\) is
214	% arbitrary, and \(G^0\) is connected.  So \(G^0\) is irreducible by
215	% \cite[6.1.10]{EGAI}.
216	% [\dots]
217	% Now, \(U\x U\) is affine, so quasi-compact.  Hence \(G^0\) is
218	% quasi-compact.  By hypothesis, \(G\) is locally of finite type. Hence
219	% \(G^0\) is of finite type.  Thus (3) holds."
220	\begin{proof}
221	  We establish
222	  \crefrange{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}
223	  together, following Kleiman's proof of Lem.~lem:agps~(3). The four
224	  paragraphs below treat, in order: the clopen-subscheme structure
225	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}); base-change commutation
226	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}); the subgroup
227	  homomorphism (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism});
228	  and finite type plus geometric irreducibility
229	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}).
230	
231	  \emph{Clopen subscheme (\cref{thm:identity_component_open_subgroup}).}
232	  Since \(e\) is a \(k\)-rational point, the singleton \(\{e\}\) is a closed
233	  point of \(G\). The connected component \(G^0\) of \(|G|\) at \(e\) is the
234	  largest connected subspace containing \(e\); the closure of a connected
235	  subspace is connected, hence \(G^0\) is closed. On the other hand, a
236	  locally Noetherian topological space has open connected components
237	  (EGA I 6.1.9), so \(G^0 \subseteq |G|\) is also open. Equip \(G^0\) with
238	  the open-subscheme structure; the inclusion
239	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is then both an open and a closed immersion.
240	
241	  \emph{Base-change commutation
242	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_base_change_commutes}).}
243	  Geometric connectedness of \(G^0\) follows from the existence of the
244	  \(k\)-rational point \(e \in G^0(k)\) (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.14); in
245	  particular for any field extension \(K/k\) the base change
246	  \((G^0)_K\) is connected, contains the image of the identity section
247	  of \(G_K\), and is open and closed in \(G_K\) (since open- and
248	  closed-immersion both descend under base change). Hence
249	  \((G^0)_K\) coincides, as a subscheme of \(G_K\), with the identity
250	  component \((G_K)^0\) of \(G_K\); the natural comparison map
251	  \((G^0)_K \to (G_K)^0\) is therefore an isomorphism.
252	
253	  \emph{Subgroup homomorphism
254	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_is_subgroup_homomorphism}).}
255	  The product \(G^0 \times_k G^0\) is connected
256	  (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.5.8). Let \(\alpha : G \times_k G \to G\),
257	  \(\alpha(g, h) := g h^{-1}\), be the algebraic-group structure map.
258	  Since \(\alpha(G^0 \times_k G^0)\) is connected and contains \(e\), its
259	  image lies in \(G^0\). Hence \(G^0\) is closed under the group
260	  operation (and under inversion, by taking \(g = e\)): the inclusion
261	  \(G^0 \hookrightarrow G\) is a homomorphism of \(k\)-group schemes,
262	  and \(G^0\) inherits a \(k\)-group-scheme structure from \(G\).
263	
264	  \emph{Finite type and geometric irreducibility
265	  (\cref{thm:identity_component_finite_type_geom_irreducible}).}
266	  To establish geometric irreducibility and finite type, base-change
267	  to \(\overline k\) (which is permitted because formation of \(G^0\)
268	  commutes with field extension by the previous paragraph). Over
269	  \(\overline k\), the product
270	  \(G^0_{\red} \times G^0_{\red}\) is reduced (EGA IV\(_2\) 4.6.1), so
271	  \(G^0_{\red}\) is itself a subgroup; we may therefore replace \(G^0\) by
272	  \(G^0_{\red}\) for the geometric-irreducibility argument. Pick a
273	  nonempty smooth affine open subscheme \(U \subseteq G^0\) (such a \(U\)
274	  exists because \(G^0_{\red}\) is reduced and \(\overline k\) is
275	  algebraically closed). For any \(\overline k\)-point \(h \in G^0\) the
276	  translate \(hg^{-1} U\) (for \(g \in U(\overline k)\)) is a smooth open
277	  neighbourhood of \(h\), so \(G^0\) is locally irreducible at every closed
278	  point. Combined with connectedness, this gives irreducibility (EGA I
279	  6.1.10). The image \(\alpha(U \times U) = G^0\) is the surjective image
280	  of the affine, hence quasi-compact, scheme \(U \times U\), so \(G^0\) is
281	  quasi-compact; together with the locally-finite-type hypothesis on
282	  \(G\), this makes \(G^0\) of finite type over \(k\).
283	\end{proof}

329	\section{The degree map}
330	\label{sec:pic_degree}
331	
332	The disjoint-union structure of \(\Pic_{C/k}\)
333	(\cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: a disjoint union of open
334	quasi-projective \(k\)-subschemes) is indexed by the Hilbert polynomial
335	\(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\) of a representing invertible sheaf
336	(\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}). For a smooth proper geometrically
337	integral curve \(C/k\) of genus \(g\) with a fixed degree-one polarisation
338	\(\mathcal{O}_C(1)\) on \(C\), the Hilbert polynomial of an invertible
339	sheaf \(\mathcal{L}\) on \(C\) is, by Riemann--Roch,
340	\(\Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n) = n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g\), so it is
341	linear in \(n\) with leading coefficient $\deg \mathcal{L} \in
342	\mathbb{Z}$. This identifies the connected components of
343	\(\Pic_{C/k}(\overline k)\) with the integers, the index being the
344	degree.
345	
346	\begin{definition}\leanok

348	  \label{def:divisor_degree_pic}

358	  % for some divisor \(D\), and \(D\) is uniquely determined up to linear
359	  % equivalence, we can define \(\deg(\mathcal{L}) = \deg(D)\). Then
360	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L}^n) = \deg(n D) = n \deg(D)\), and the Riemann-Roch
361	  % theorem says that \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n) = n \deg(\mathcal{L}) + 1 - g\).
362	  % This gives a more canonical description of \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\): when
363	  % \(\chi(C, \mathcal{L}^n)\) is written as a polynomial in \(n\),
364	  % \(\deg(\mathcal{L})\) is the leading coefficient. We write
365	  % \(\Pic^0(C)\) for the group of isomorphism classes of invertible sheaves

380	  \[
381	    \Phi_{\mathcal{L}}(n)
382	    \;=\; \chi(C, \mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{O}_C(n))
383	    \;=\; n \cdot \deg \mathcal{L} + 1 - g,
384	  \]
385	  a polynomial in \(n\) linear in \(n\). The integer
386	  \(\deg(\lambda) := \deg \mathcal{L}\) is the leading coefficient of
387	  this polynomial, well-defined on the isomorphism class
388	  \([\mathcal{L}]\) and (because \(\Pic_{C/k}\) represents the
389	  \'etale-sheafified relative Picard functor) on the \(k\)-point
390	  \(\lambda\). The degree map is a group homomorphism: for
391	  \(\mathcal{L}, \mathcal{M}\) invertible sheaves on \(C\) one has
392	  \(\deg(\mathcal{L} \otimes \mathcal{M}) = \deg \mathcal{L} + \deg \mathcal{M}\),
393	  because the Euler characteristic is additive on the additive groups of
394	  twists. Equivalently, the degree on \(k\)-points is the index of the
395	  connected component of \(\Pic_{C/k}\) containing \(\lambda\) under the
396	  Hilbert-polynomial decomposition of
397	  \cref{thm:fga_pic_representability}: the disjoint union
398	  \(\Pic_{C/k} = \coprod_{d \in \mathbb{Z}} \Pic^d_{C/k}\) is indexed by
399	  the degree, with \(\Pic^d_{C/k}\) the component representing classes
400	  of degree \(d\).
401	\end{definition}

403	\section{The Pic-zero abelian variety}
404	\label{sec:pic0_abelian_variety}
405	
406	The terminal statement of the chapter identifies \(\Pic^0_{C/k}\) with
407	an abelian variety of dimension \(g\) --- the Jacobian variety of \(C\).
408	Recall (Milne~\S I.1, p.~8) that a \(k\)-group scheme is an
409	\emph{abelian variety} iff it is a complete (proper) connected group
410	variety over \(k\); commutativity and projectivity then follow
411	automatically.

500

502	  % \S III.1, p.~88; verbatim quote reproduced at

508	  Let \(C/k\) be a smooth proper geometrically integral curve of genus
509	  \(g\) over a field \(k\). A \(k\)-point
510	  \(\lambda \in \Pic_{C/k}(k)\) lies in the image of the inclusion of
511	  the identity component \(\Pic^0_{C/k} \hookrightarrow \Pic_{C/k}\)
